Validator confirmed #M5 (healthcheck test os.Exit hazard): dormant only because makeNode() always sets the same address; NodeEligible()/NodeManager methods have no nil-receiver guard, so a hypothetical construction failure would panic rather than fail cleanly.

Node-delete watch event previously drove two non-fatal handlers (log-only + mark-ineligible); NodeManager.OnNodeDelete now unconditionally exitFunc(1)s with no comment/commit/issue explaining why; doc comment doesn't mention deletion is fatal at all.

Node informer is Start()ed and synced inside newNodeManager during newProxyServer, before NodeConfig's handlers are registered in Run(); the old explicit ordering comment was deleted without replacement, and NodeConfig has no AddFunc to catch the replay.

s.podCIDRs field comment '// only used for LocalModeNodeCIDR' is now inaccurate; PodCIDRs() returns Spec.PodCIDRs unconditionally, reactivating dead validation code for non-NodeCIDR default mode; corroborated by the deleted TestProxyServer_platformSetup.

NodeEligible() now deep-copies the whole Node object per /healthz call to read only DeletionTimestamp and Spec.Taints; Node() has exactly one caller in the whole tree, so every call pays for a full-object copy that's discarded immediately.

hs.lock in NodeEligible() is vestigial — the field it used to guard (nodeEligible bool) is gone, and NodeEligible() now reads NodeManager's own mutex-guarded state, so the exclusive lock only adds unnecessary contention.
PodCIDRs() returns a live reference to the informer-owned slice while Node() explicitly deep-copies — asymmetric encapsulation; benign today but a latent trap for a future mutating caller.
NodeIPs() computing fresh from n.node under the mutex is safe; GetNodeHostIPs allocates a new slice each call, no aliasing concern.

NodeTopologyConfig's late handler registration is fine — unlike NodeConfig it does wire a real AddFunc (handleNodeEvent), so the late-registration replay correctly delivers current topology labels at startup.

checkBadIPConfig's podCIDR fatal-error path confirmed to still trigger fatal=true only for LocalModeNodeCIDR, so the reactivated validation logic can only produce non-fatal warnings, not startup failures.
winkernel/metaproxier/hollow-proxy OnTopologyChange wiring is mechanically consistent; compile-time var _ proxy.Provider assertions still enforce the new interface — no divergence found.

os.Exit reachability for hollow-proxy (kubemark): HollowProxy constructs ProxyServer directly bypassing newProxyServer, so NodeManager/HealthzServer stay nil and Run()'s guard prevents any nil-dereference.

5-minute PodCIDR wait timeout itself existed pre-PR; what's new is the same ceiling now also governs the NodeIPs-only wait (previously ~30-60s) — trades a silent old fallback for a loud new one, looks deliberate but undocumented as a trade-off.

klog.Flush() vs old klog.FlushAndExit(klog.ExitFlushTimeout,1) assessed by broad-reviewer as functionally comparable with no observable behavior difference — contradicted by several other reviewers' High findings on the same point.

Positive: consolidating three previously-separate node informers/handlers into a single field-selected, mutex-guarded NodeManager is a meaningful simplification, removing duplicated diffing logic across iptables/ipvs/nftables proxiers.

Positive: switching getNodeIPs' direct-Get() polling to reading off nodeLister (local informer cache) is a genuine efficiency improvement — increased poll frequency doesn't translate into extra apiserver load.
Positive: NodeEligible() moving from a cached, push-updated bool to a live pull off NodeManager.Node() is a solid fix for cached-flag drift; mutex discipline within NodeManager is consistent.

Positive: test coverage for the new NodeManager (TestNewNodeManager, TestNodeManagerOnNodeChange, TestNodeManagerOnNodeDelete, TestNodeManagerNode) is clear and exercises poll/timeout/exit paths directly with a fake clientset.

Validator confirmed #H5 (tautological test): independently re-derived the tautology; argued High severity is defensible since a regression here would be a silent perf regression with zero other test coverage.

Validator confirmed #H2 (klog.Flush unbounded): verified via vendored klog source that FlushAndExit's goroutine+timeout race exists specifically because flush hooks may deadlock/take too long; plain Flush() has no such bound.

Note on suggested fix: klog.FlushAndExit unconditionally calls the package-global klog.OsExit, bypassing the injectable exitFunc that tests rely on for isolation; a correct fix needs a bounded-flush-then-call-exitFunc pattern, not a literal substitution.

Validator confirmed #M3 (5-min comment): verified pre-PR getNodeIPs used ~30s backoff, non-fatal; confirmed via gh api that adrianmoisey's PR thread comment is anchored at this line; author replied conversationally but no code/comment change was made.

[PERF_HOT_PATH] NodeEligible(), invoked on every /healthz request (kubelet liveness ~10s + external-LB health checks), now does a full v1.Node DeepCopy() moved from the infrequent node-event path onto the frequent per-request healthz path; also takes hs.lock.Lock() (exclusive).

reflect.DeepEqual on []net.IP/podCIDRs per node event in OnNodeChange: slices are tiny and Node update events are infrequent (kubelet heartbeats go via Lease) — negligible cost, suppressed.
NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent's fresh map + reflect.DeepEqual per node event: map holds at most one key today, O(1) on a tiny structure — not a nameable cost at realistic scale, suppressed.
Poll timeout increase from 30s to 5 minutes only affects kube-proxy startup latency in a pathological case, not steady-state cost — out of scope for performance persona (cold path), not flagged.

[ADV_CASCADE] Node status update with no derivable host IP stores the IP-less node and early-returns without exiting, poisoning the baseline; a subsequent update restoring the original IP triggers os.Exit(1) even though net NodeIP is unchanged (X→∅→X).

[ADV_ABUSE] Any Node-object delete event now os.Exit(1)s kube-proxy; trigger fans out with cardinality — node re-registration crashes that proxy, a bulk/DR event crashes the whole fleet simultaneously, and synchronized restarts thunder the API server.

[ADV_ASSUMPTION] newProxyServer now hard-fails when the node has no derivable host IP within 5 min; old getNodeIPs/detectNodeIPs' BindAddress fallback is now unreachable for IP-less nodes — a bare-metal node relying on that fallback previously ran degraded, now crash-loops.

[ADV_CASCADE] Crash path's klog.Flush() blocks with no timeout if the log sink is blocked (disk pressure, full stderr — exactly the conditions under which a node misbehaves); crash-restart recovery silently fails to fire. [Inference/Unverified]

newNodeManager returning (nil, nil) → nil-deref hypothesis fell apart on construction: every return-false path assigns non-nil err first, and PollUntilContextCancel(immediate=true) runs the closure at least once before any context check.

hs.nodeManager nil in NodeEligible()/n.node nil in Node() hypothesis: not reachable — NewProxyHealthServer only constructed with a non-nil, error-checked NodeManager; hollow-proxy never constructs a HealthzServer.
-race between the informer handler goroutine and healthz HTTP handler: clean — n.node access fully guarded by n.mu, Node() returns a DeepCopy, lock ordering consistent, no data race constructible.

Missing AddFunc on NewNodeConfig assessed as intentional and safe by adversarial-reviewer: NodeManager already captured the baseline node during construction; subsequent changes arrive via UpdateFunc→OnNodeChange. Contradicted by most other reviewers' High findings.
NodeEligible() switched from RLock to Lock is a minor concurrency reduction (serializes concurrent /healthz reads), not a correctness issue.

[DECISION_LOG_MISSING] Crash-on-change lifecycle (NodeIPs/PodCIDRs/deletion) rationale is unrecorded; the one breadcrumb that existed (issue 111321) was deleted from the entire tree, and NodeManager's type doc omits the node-deletion exit entirely.

[LLM_COMPREHENSION_RISK] The 5-minute poll-timeout comment attributes it solely to PodCIDR, but it now also bounds NodeIP waiting (was ~30s, non-fatal) and makes startup fatal — comment points a maintainer away from the value's true dominant purpose.

[ASSUMPTION_UNVALIDATED] The topology-label filtering invariant between CategorizeEndpoints and handleNodeEvent is documented only from topology.go's side; the config.go filter site (where a developer would actually edit it) has no reciprocal comment.

[LLM_COMPREHENSION_RISK] Silent switch from klog.FlushAndExit(timeout, code) to klog.Flush()+injected exitFunc drops the bounded-flush guarantee with no note explaining the trade-off.

ProxyHealthServer doc comment item 3 ('sync node status ... if marked for deletion') — mechanism changed from push to live pull; 'sync' is now a mild misnomer but not misleading about behavior — minor wording drift.

nodeManager nil-deref risk in NodeEligible(): reads hs.nodeManager.Node() unconditionally; hollow-proxy leaves NodeManager nil — whether the health server is ever constructed on that path is out of knowledge-reviewer's scope.
Lock ordering hs.lock → nodeManager.mu in NodeEligible/Node examined: OnNodeChange takes only nodeManager.mu, no reverse acquisition, no cycle — not a comprehension risk.

topology.go comment imprecision ('watch other endpoint types or labels', double space) — the filter concerns node labels not endpoint types; cosmetic, folded into the one-sided-invariant finding rather than flagged separately.
winkernel OnTopologyChange no-op with TODO(imroc): intent marker present and clear, no knowledge loss.
Removed NoopNodeHandler/getNodeIPs/waitForPodCIDR and their tests: dead code removed as part of consolidation, no false context left behind — not flagged.

[API_CONTRACT] NodeConfig wires only UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc, omitting AddFunc, so no handler observes 'creation' events; late-registration replay is silently dropped, and NodeHandler's doc ('creation or modification') no longer matches the wiring.

[API_CONTRACT] handleChangeNode's tombstone-unwrapping branch is unreachable dead code on the update path (tombstones only arrive via delete deltas); misleading defensive construct paired oddly with the dropped AddFunc.

[DATA_MODEL] PodCIDRs() returns n.node.Spec.PodCIDRs directly, aliasing the informer-owned Node object, whereas Node() DeepCopy()s and NodeIPs() allocates fresh — inconsistent defensive-copy contract, a 'never mutate lister-returned objects' hazard.

[RESILIENCE_GAP] OnNodeDelete now calls exitFunc(1), a hard crash on Node-delete observation, replacing the prior graceful-ineligibility-and-drain behavior; a transient/spurious delete now crashes kube-proxy instead of degrading, risking CrashLoopBackOff on flapping.

[API_CONTRACT] ProxyServer.NodeManager has an ambiguous nil-ness contract: Run() guards 'if s.NodeManager != nil' yet NewProxyHealthServer/NodeEligible() assume non-nil with no nil check — no live crash found today, but the invariant is inconsistent across call sites.
Lock ordering hs.lock → nodeManager.mu (deadlock risk): sound, no code path acquires the reverse order — NodeManager never calls into the health server; no lock cycle.

Import cycle from the healthcheck→proxy dependency flip: verified no cycle (pkg/proxy no longer imports pkg/proxy/healthcheck); coupling direction now reasonable, though the seam is held only by convention.

NodeIPs() aliasing concern: not an issue — GetNodeHostIPs allocates fresh net.IP values via ParseIPSloppy and a new slice, does not alias informer-owned memory (only PodCIDRs() does, flagged separately).
exitFunc injection/testability: good design — the os.Exit seam is injected via newNodeManager(..., exitFunc, ...) and tests substitute a capturing func; the crash mechanism is cleanly testable.

os.Exit vs old klog.FlushAndExit assessed as acceptable by design-reviewer: new code calls klog.Flush() immediately before exitFunc(1), preserving log-flush-before-exit behavior — contradicted by several other reviewers' High findings on unbounded flush.

OnTopologyChange narrowing the proxier contract (whole *v1.Node → topology labels) assessed as sound and an improvement; topology.go carries a cross-linking comment to NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent, keeping the two sites coherent.

Validator confirmed #M1 (NodeEligible deep-copy/lock): confirmed hs.lock previously guarded a legitimate cached bool, now provably vestigial. Recommends Medium severity (bounded by ~10s healthz polling) rather than performance-reviewer's High.

[BUG_LOGIC] NewNodeConfig no longer wires an AddFunc, so NodeManager silently misses client-go's 'replay current store state' notification for a late-joining handler, permanently losing any Node change between NodeManager's initial poll and this registration.

[BUG_LOGIC] klog.Flush() (lines 154, 170, 178) blocks indefinitely on I/O with no timeout, unlike the klog.FlushAndExit idiom used elsewhere for fatal exits; if the flush stalls, exitFunc(1) is never reached.

[BUG_CONCURRENCY] NodeEligible() still takes hs.lock.Lock() (full write lock) even though the field it protected (nodeEligible) was removed; now only reads via hs.nodeManager.Node() (its own mutex), needlessly serializing /healthz and /livez.

[QUALITY_DUPLICATION] handleChangeNode's DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone-handling branch is unreachable — copy-pasted from handleDeleteNode but wired only to UpdateFunc, where client-go never dispatches tombstones.

'if pollErr != nil { return nil, err }' verified correct, not a bug: the polled closure always sets a non-nil err before any (false, nil) return; intentional per adjacent comment and matches test's specific-message assertions.

Goroutine 'leak' from thisNodeInformerFactory.Start(wait.NeverStop) on poll failure: real but matches a pre-existing codebase idiom; in production a failed newProxyServer normally leads to process exit anyway — not worth a hard finding.

OnNodeChange mutates n.node before deciding whether to crash: a narrow window between n.mu.Unlock() and exitFunc(1) actually terminating could let a concurrent caller observe about-to-be-fatal state; in production exitFunc is os.Exit so the window is negligible.
Shared topologyLabels map reference passed to multiple OnTopologyChange handlers: checked all consumers — none mutate the map in place, only read — no aliasing corruption risk.

ProxyHealthServer/NodeManager nil-pointer risk for HollowProxy: HollowProxy never sets NodeManager or HealthzServer, and Run()'s guard plus serveHealthz's nil handling prevent a crash; no reachable nil-deref on the real path.

[GO_GOROUTINES] NewNodeConfig registers only UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc on an informer already started+synced earlier in NewNodeManager(); a late-registered handler replays the current store as synthetic Add notifications, a no-op when AddFunc is nil.

[GO_GOROUTINES] newNodeManager unconditionally calls thisNodeInformerFactory.Start(wait.NeverStop); correct for production but the same function is invoked from ~12 unit-test call sites, each leaving reflector/processor goroutines never told to stop.

[GO_ERRORS][GO_NIL] nodeManager, _ := proxy.NewNodeManager(...) discards the constructor's error (also line 561); if NewNodeManager ever fails here, nodeManager is nil and none of NodeManager's methods guard against a nil receiver.

[GO_DEFER] OnNodeChange/OnNodeDelete call klog.Flush() (unbounded, synchronous) followed by a separate exitFunc(1), duplicating crash-on-change behavior that used klog.FlushAndExit(klog.ExitFlushTimeout, 1), which bounds the flush with a 10s goroutine+select.

[GO_MEMORY_MODEL] PodCIDRs() returns n.node.Spec.PodCIDRs directly under the lock — the raw slice header aliased into the same *v1.Node the informer delivered; Node() explicitly DeepCopy()s for exactly this reason but PodCIDRs() doesn't.

[GO_CONTEXT] NodeManager accepts ctx but never derives/stores a contextual klog.Logger from it, unlike every sibling type (NodeConfig, NodeTopologyConfig, ServiceCIDRConfig); OnNodeChange/OnNodeDelete call global klog.InfoS/ErrorS directly instead.

[GO_CONTEXT] Uses context.TODO() for proxy.NewNodeManager (also line 561); this file has no ktesting import at all, unlike node_test.go/config_test.go which consistently use ktesting.NewTestContext(t).

[GO_GOROUTINES] TestNewNodeManager synchronizes a background goroutine's client mutations with the poll loop purely via time.Sleep(100ms) then time.Sleep(15ms) against a 10ms poll interval — a wall-clock race under CI scheduling jitter.

err/pollErr invariant in newNodeManager verified against vendored loopConditionUntilContext: with immediate=true, condition() runs at least once before any ctx.Done() check, and every closure return path sets err first — a fragile implicit contract but not a live bug as written.

cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone branch inside handleChangeNode (wired only to UpdateFunc) confirmed defensive dead code — tombstones are synthesized exclusively on the delete path, never delivered to OnUpdate.

n.node = node committed before validating the new node's IPs parse: a real ordering smell, but NodeIPs()/PodCIDRs() have no callers after server startup, so there is currently no live consumer that would observe the bad intermediate state.
os.Exit called from an informer event-handler goroutine (not main) is safe in Go — terminates the whole process immediately regardless of caller; no special handling required.

reflect.DeepEqual(oldPodCIDRs, node.Spec.PodCIDRs) nil-vs-empty-slice edge case could theoretically cause a spurious 'changed' detection if API server (de)serialization ever produced a non-nil empty slice instead of nil — too speculative to confirm.
context.TODO() in hollow_proxy.go:90 confirmed via diff to be untouched by this PR (only FakeProxier's methods changed in that file) — pre-existing, out of scope.

Double exitFunc(1) invocation if both PodCIDRs and NodeIPs change in the same OnNodeChange call: in production os.Exit on the first call already terminates the process, so the second call is unreachable; no observable defect.

NewNodeConfig wires only UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc, no AddFunc, so a late-registered handler's synthetic Add replay of the informer's current cache is silently dropped, delaying detection of a NodeIP/PodCIDR change that lands in the startup window.

klog.Flush() replaces klog.FlushAndExit() on crash paths; the plain Flush is unbounded/synchronous so a stalled log sink can prevent os.Exit from ever firing, silently disabling the crash-restart safety net.

NewNodeConfig no longer wires AddFunc, breaking NodeHandler.OnNodeChange's documented creation contract; the sibling NewNodeTopologyConfig added in the same PR correctly wires AddFunc, suggesting an oversight.

NodeEligible() takes an unnecessary exclusive lock shared with the proxier sync loop's bookkeeping and deep-copies the whole Node object on every call, where the previous code read a cached bool under RLock.
The https://issues.k8s.io/111321 rationale link explaining why kube-proxy must crash on PodCIDR reassignment was dropped with no replacement, though recoverable via git history.
NodeConfig's handler registers only UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc on an informer NodeManager already started and synced minutes earlier; a change landing in the gap window is dropped as a silent Add replay.

In OnNodeChange, when watchPodCIDRs is true and PodCIDRs changed, exitFunc(1) is called without an early return, so execution falls through to also evaluate NodeIPs and potentially call exitFunc(1) twice.
handleChangeNode's DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone-recovery branch is unreachable since it's wired only to UpdateFunc, which client-go never delivers a tombstone to.

NodeManager's constructor takes a ctx but never derives a contextual logger from it, using global klog calls instead, unlike every sibling Config type in the same PR that stores klog.FromContext(ctx).
handleNodeEvent's doc comment claims to handle Add, Update and Delete events, copying the sibling convention, but its own DeleteFunc registration is a no-op that never calls it.

thisNodeInformerFactory.Start(wait.NeverStop) runs before cache-sync succeeds; on either error return the reflector goroutines and API watch are never stopped, confirmed empirically via go test -race showing leaked goroutines on error-path subtests.

if pollErr != nil { return nil, err } discards the real poll-level error (e.g. context.Canceled) in favor of the last domain-specific condition error, misleading callers on genuine cancellation versus a data problem.

Dead code: unreachable tombstone branch in handleChangeNode, unused listerSynced field, and unused nodeLister field

The PR is a merged, well-structured cleanup; the Provider/NodeTopologyHandler interface narrowing is praised by multiple agents, with issue #1 (NodeIP-loss ordering in OnNodeChange) the one item worth real scrutiny
Genuine consolidation: ~800 lines of duplicated per-proxier node handling collapse into one NodeManager and NodeTopologyConfig; Provider interface correctly narrowed.
Topology filter is a real efficiency win: handleNodeEvent now filters to only LabelTopologyZone so churn on unrelated node labels no longer forces a full proxier resync.
Good cross-reference discipline: comment added to CategorizeEndpoints explicitly points at the NodeTopologyConfig filter to keep the two in sync.
Testability designed in: injected exitFunc/pollInterval/pollTimeout/callback; node_test.go table tests cover poll-for-node/PodCIDR/exit-on-change matrix and tombstone handling.
Build and touched tests pass: go build/go vet clean, go test -race green for pkg/proxy/config, pkg/proxy, and pkg/proxy/healthcheck.

Unsynchronized eventHandlers slice: handleNodeEvent reads it on the informer goroutine while RegisterEventHandler appends on the main goroutine with no mutex, a confirmed data race that can also silently drop the proxier's initial topology labels.

Behavior change: NodeManager.OnNodeDelete now calls exitFunc(1) (os.Exit) on node deletion, whereas previously deletion drained gracefully (nodeEligible=false / logged) — an undocumented semantic change.
NodeEligible() takes a full write Lock() though it mutates nothing, and dereferences hs.nodeManager.Node() without a nil guard.
OnNodeChange can invoke exitFunc twice in one call (node.go:155 and 171); harmless with real os.Exit but masks double-invocation with an injected test exitFunc.
Healthcheck tests use the real os.Exit via proxy.NewNodeManager instead of an injected exitFunc, so a future edit could kill the test process instead of failing cleanly.
OnNodeChange stores the informer-owned *v1.Node directly in n.node without a DeepCopy; safe under current invariants but an implicit assumption worth documenting.

Lost integration coverage: nothing now covers the server.Run() wiring where the eventHandlers race lives; a -race test with production ordering (informer synced before RegisterEventHandler) would catch it.

The 5-minute pollTimeout comment claims it's solely for PodCIDR allocation, but it now covers node-existence and NodeIP readiness too (and applies even when watchPodCIDRs is false).
NodeManager's type doc comment has a duplicated 'handles' verb with no punctuation, garbling the sentence (merge artifact from two absorbed structs).
OnNodeChange's doc comment claims it fires on 'creation and update', but NewNodeConfig only registers UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc (no AddFunc), so it's never invoked for node creation in production.

OnNodeChange commits the incoming node to n.node before validating its IPs; a GetNodeHostIPs failure is only logged, leaving the invalid node as baseline and poisoning the next change-detection comparison, causing a spurious crash later.

The new startup poll loop in newNodeManager retries silently on every failure with zero per-attempt logging, unlike the deleted getNodeIPs which logged every failed attempt — a real observability regression during multi-minute startup stalls.
On poll timeout, newNodeManager returns the last captured validation error instead of pollErr, so a context cancellation during startup is misreported as a persistent node-config problem.
No test case exercises watchPodCIDRs=false with an actual PodCIDR change to confirm the crash-suppression gate works; a regression inverting the guard would go undetected.
Dual-stack NodeIP reordering is untested; OnNodeChange's order-sensitive DeepEqual comparison could spuriously crash on a benign address reorder with no test locking in the intended behavior.
The cache.WaitForNamedCacheSync failure branch in newNodeManager is not exercised by any test.
OnNodeChange's transient-error path (GetNodeHostIPs failing on the incoming node, logged and returned without exit) is untested.

config.NodeConfig's informer-to-handler dispatch layer (handleChangeNode/handleDeleteNode/Run) has zero unit test coverage, despite this PR changing it non-trivially (merged Add+Update, added tombstone fallback, dropped AddFunc).
Dual-stack PodCIDRs are no longer explicitly tested after the deletion of TestProxyServer_platformSetup's two-CIDR case; TestNewNodeManager only ever supplies a single CIDR.

NewNodeConfig's ResourceEventHandlerFuncs only wires UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc, not AddFunc, so a late-registered handler on an already-synced informer never receives the replayed initial-state Add, creating a startup-window blind spot for NodeManager's crash-on-change safety.
pkg/proxy/config/config_test.go has no test at all for NodeConfig/NodeHandler, so the missing-AddFunc regression (and other NodeConfig wiring changes in this PR) would not be caught by any test.

NodeManager.PodCIDRs() returns the raw informer-owned slice directly, unlike the sibling Node() accessor which deep-copies — an inconsistent encapsulation discipline that risks corrupting shared/informer state if a caller mutates the result.
OnNodeChange stores the incoming node into n.node before validating its host IPs; on validation failure it only logs, leaving n.node in a state that violates the constructor-enforced invariant.

NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent passes its internal topologyLabels map to every registered handler by reference rather than a defensive copy; safe today only by convention (nothing currently mutates it).
ProxyHealthServer.NodeEligible() still takes the hs.lock write lock even though the field it used to guard (nodeEligible) was removed — needless lock contention with no correctness benefit.
NodeManager.nodeLister field is written during construction but never read afterward — dead struct state.
New self-inflicted os.Exit(1) paths (NodeIP change, PodCIDR change, node delete) emit no metric or Event before exiting, so a fleet-wide correlated crashloop would be operationally invisible.

The behavior change of hard-crashing on node deletion and NodeIP change (previously tolerated, node-delete only set 503) is undocumented in the NodeManager type comment and ships as /kind cleanup with no visible release note.
n.node baseline is replaced before the new node's IPs are validated, so one malformed/transient update primes a spurious later exit.

Change detection keys on derived IP ordering via reflect.DeepEqual, so a benign address reorder (e.g. after kubelet restart) is treated as an IP change and crashes kube-proxy even though the IP set is unchanged.

Startup now hard-fails (blocking up to a hardcoded 5 minutes, in all modes) when the node lacks NodeIPs, replacing the previous best-effort ~63s-backoff startup that degraded gracefully instead of aborting.
NodeTopologyConfig.listerSynced is set but never read — no Run/sync-gating exists for this type, unlike its siblings.
handleChangeNode (wired only as UpdateFunc) contains a dead DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone-handling branch copy-pasted from the delete handler that can never execute.
No return statement after exitFunc(1) on the PodCIDR-change path in OnNodeChange; harmless with real os.Exit but could call exitFunc twice with an injected non-terminating exit function.

NewNodeConfig's ResourceEventHandlerFuncs only wires UpdateFunc and DeleteFunc, not AddFunc — dropped when OnNodeAdd/OnNodeUpdate were merged into OnNodeChange — contradicting OnNodeChange's documented 'creation or modification' contract.

s.podCIDRs is now populated unconditionally regardless of DetectLocalMode, causing checkBadIPConfig's non-fatal podCIDR-family warning to fire for modes (ClusterCIDR, BridgeInterface, etc.) where that field is irrelevant.

NodeConfig's node-informer registration omits AddFunc, so client-go's late-join replay (delivered because NewNodeManager now starts/syncs the informer before NodeConfig registers its handler) is silently dropped, meaning node state changes in the startup window never reach NodeManager.OnNodeChange.

No security vulnerabilities found (NONE verdict): the crash-on-change surface is per-node-scoped (field-selector isolation) and requires privileged NodeRestriction/RBAC-gated mutation, making it an availability tradeoff rather than a security hole; no injection sinks, no new RBAC surface, no reachable nil-deref or data race.

ProxyHealthServer depends on the concrete *proxy.NodeManager but uses only its Node() method, inverting the prior proxy→healthcheck dependency direction and forcing tests to stand up a full informer-backed NodeManager.

Crash-on-change semantics broadened undocumented: NodeIP change and node deletion now both call exitFunc(1), whereas previously IP changes were never re-evaluated and node deletion only flipped health status to 503 while the proxy kept serving.

NewNodeManager now blocks proxy startup up to 5 minutes on NodeIP availability and hard-fails on timeout in all modes, converting the old 'start degraded with fallback IPs' behavior into a blocking/crashlooping one.

Informer start ordering now inverts a previously documented invariant (informer starts inside NewNodeManager before NodeConfig registers handlers), and NodeConfig no longer registers AddFunc, silently dropping the initial-state replay — undocumented and fragile for future maintainers.
NodeEligible() now acquires hs.lock.Lock() (write lock) even though the field it used to guard (nodeEligible) is gone and it only reads hs.nodeManager.Node() — superfluous locking.
NodeConfig.handleChangeNode contains a DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone branch that is unreachable since it's only wired to UpdateFunc.
PodCIDRs() returns n.node.Spec.PodCIDRs without copying — safe today only because OnNodeChange replaces the whole pointer rather than mutating in place, but is a shared-backing-array aliasing risk.
handleChangeNode (registered only as UpdateFunc) contains an unreachable tombstone-unwrapping branch for cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown, a pattern only relevant to delete handlers.
NodeTopologyConfig.listerSynced is assigned but never consumed anywhere — no Run() method exists to wait on it, unlike sibling NodeConfig.

Health-check unit tests construct NodeManager via the public constructor which hard-codes os.Exit as its failure path, unlike node_test.go's injectable unexported constructor — a future test edit could kill the test binary instead of failing cleanly.
ProxyHealthServer.NodeEligible()/Node() unconditionally dereference nodeManager, while server.go's own comment acknowledges a nil NodeManager is a real, expected state for hollow-proxy.
NodeManager's doc comment does not mention that it also exits unconditionally on node deletion (a third, undocumented crash trigger) and is grammatically malformed from a merge artifact.
ProxyHealthServer.NodeEligible() still takes hs.lock even though it no longer touches any state that lock protects, unnecessarily serializing against other lock users.

[H1] NewNodeConfig drops AddFunc, so the initial-state informer replay is silently discarded and node changes in the startup window bypass NodeManager's crash-on-change safety (converged by 5 reviewers).
[H2] All three os.Exit(1) paths (NodeIP change, PodCIDR change, node delete) emit no metric or Event before exiting, so fleet-wide kube-proxy crashloops are operationally invisible.
[M1] OnNodeChange overwrites n.node before validating the new node's IPs; a transient IP-less update poisons the baseline and causes a later spurious crash.
[M2] NodeIP change detection uses order-sensitive reflect.DeepEqual on a derived slice, so benign address reordering triggers a spurious crash.
[M3] Node deletion and NodeIP change now os.Exit(1) in all modes (previously delete→503 drain, IP change ignored) — undocumented, no release note, and the type doc omits the delete-crash.
[M4] s.podCIDRs is now populated unconditionally, so checkBadIPConfig's podCIDR-family warning can fire for non-LocalModeNodeCIDR modes where the field is irrelevant.
[M5] NewNodeManager now blocks startup up to a hardcoded 5 min and hard-fails kube-proxy when NodeIPs aren't ready, in all detect-local modes — previously best-effort start with fallback.
[M6] healthcheck now depends on the concrete *proxy.NodeManager but uses only Node(), inverting the package dependency direction and forcing heavy test setup.
[M7] The startup poll loop dropped all per-attempt diagnostic logging, causing multi-minute silent startup stalls with no operator visibility.
[M8] NewNodeManager's '5 minutes for PodCIDR' comment is now inaccurate since the same timeout covers node existence and NodeIP readiness too.

[M9] config.NodeConfig's informer→handler dispatch has zero test coverage before or after this PR, despite this PR changing it non-trivially (merged Add+Update, added tombstone branch, dropped AddFunc).
[M10] watchPodCIDRs=false with a PodCIDR change is never tested, so a regression in the if n.watchPodCIDRs guard would crashloop every non-LocalModeNodeCIDR deployment undetected.
[M11] healthcheck_test.go builds its NodeManager via the public NewNodeManager, which hard-wires the real os.Exit, so a future test edit could kill the test binary instead of failing.
[L1] NodeEligible() takes the hs.lock write lock but no longer guards any state it protects, causing needless contention with Updated()/QueuedUpdate().

[L2] PodCIDRs() returns the raw informer-owned slice without copying, while sibling Node() deliberately DeepCopy()s — inconsistent encapsulation risking corrupted comparison baseline or shared informer state.
[L3] NodeManager type doc comment is grammatically garbled with a duplicated 'handles' verb, a merge artifact from the two structs it absorbed.
[L4] NodeTopologyConfig.listerSynced is assigned but never read — no Run waits on it, unlike every sibling config type — a dead field inviting a false assumption of sync-gating.
[L5] NodeManager.nodeLister field is written at construction but never read afterward — dead struct state.
[L6] Dead cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone branch in handleChangeNode — the function is wired only to UpdateFunc, whose newObj is never a tombstone.
[L7] No return after exitFunc(1) on the PodCIDR-change path — harmless in production but with an injected non-terminating exitFunc, execution falls through and can call exitFunc twice.

[L8] NodeTopologyConfig hands its internal topologyLabels map to every handler by reference (fanned out to both family proxiers via metaProxier) — safe today only by convention, not enforced by the type.
[L9] On poll timeout, newNodeManager returns the captured business err, not pollErr, so a context cancellation racing startup is misreported as a persistent node-config problem.
[L10] Cache-sync-failure path untested — newNodeManager's 'can not sync node informer' branch has no test.
[L11] Transient GetNodeHostIPs error path untested — no OnNodeChange case sends an address-less update to confirm it doesn't crash or poison the baseline.
[L12] Dual-stack PodCIDRs no longer explicitly tested — the deleted TestProxyServer_platformSetup had a two-CIDR case; TestNewNodeManager only supplies a single CIDR.
[L13] OnNodeChange doc says it fires on 'creation and update,' but only UpdateFunc is wired — creation is actually captured by NewNodeManager's bootstrap poll.

Security Analysis returned NONE as a substantive result: crash-on-change is per-node-scoped (field-selector isolation) and requires privileged NodeRestriction/RBAC-gated mutation, an availability tradeoff rather than a security hole; no injection sink, no new RBAC surface, no reachable nil-deref or data race.

Positive: real consolidation of four overlapping node-watch mechanisms (getNodeIPs backoff, waitForPodCIDR watch, NodePodCIDRHandler, NodeEligibleHandler) plus per-proxier handlers into one NodeManager + focused NodeTopologyConfig, cutting apiserver load.

Positive: exitFunc/pollInterval/pollTimeout (and newNodeTopologyConfig's completion callback) are dependency-injected, replacing the old fragile klog.OsExit/panic-based exit testing with clean, table-driven, non-flaky tests.

Positive: NodeTopologyConfig responsibly narrows topology notifications to only LabelTopologyZone and suppresses no-op notifications, with a cross-package comment keeping topology.go and config.go in sync.

Positive: clean interface segregation splitting NodeTopologyHandler out of NodeHandler gives each Proxier a narrow OnTopologyChange(map[string]string) instead of the full node object, applied consistently across all five proxier backends.

NodeTopologyConfig registers proxier handler on an already-started/synced informer; async synthetic Add replay races with RegisterEventHandler causing unsynchronized read/append of eventHandlers/topologyLabels (data race) and can leave proxier with topologyLabels==nil forever, silently disabling zone-aware routing.
NodeConfig has no AddFunc; a NodeIP change during the startup window (between NewNodeManager's poll and handler registration) is silently swallowed since OnNodeChange never fires for it.

New self-induced restart behavior (exitFunc(1) on NodeIP/PodCIDR change or delete) ships with no metric or Kubernetes Event, only a klog line; a fleet-wide trigger would restart kube-proxy cluster-wide with no aggregated signal.
TestNewNodeTopologyConfig registers the handler before Start(), the opposite of production ordering, so it never exercises the data race or lost-initial-label scenario.

NodeEligible() switched from RLock+cached bool to a write Lock plus a full Node DeepCopy on every health/liveness probe though only DeletionTimestamp/Taints are read - wasteful hot-path allocation and serializes probes against Health().

Doc comments on NodeIPs()/PodCIDRs() say values are 'polled in NewNodeManager()' but OnNodeChange overwrites n.node on every event, so they actually return the latest node's values; NodeManager type comment is also a broken run-on sentence.
Dead DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone branch in handleChangeNode is unreachable since handleChangeNode is wired only to UpdateFunc, which never delivers tombstones.

Spurious restart possible on equivalent-but-reordered NodeIPs: OnNodeChange uses reflect.DeepEqual over an ordered slice, so reordered addresses of the same IP set could trigger an unnecessary exit(1).
NodeEligible() dereferences hs.nodeManager with no nil guard; currently guaranteed non-nil by construction so latent rather than live.

NewNodeConfig omits AddFunc entirely; verified against vendored client-go that a handler registered on an already-started informer receives a synthetic replayed Add which is a hard no-op when AddFunc is nil, so it's swallowed with zero logging/error/trace.
Both OnNodeChange (old node) and NodeIPs() discard the error from GetNodeHostIPs; asymmetric since the new-node error a few lines later in OnNodeChange is checked and logged.

No return after exitFunc(1) in the PodCIDR-drift branch of OnNodeChange; execution falls through and could call exitFunc(1) a second time for the NodeIPs check. Harmless in production since exitFunc is os.Exit, but exitFunc is an injectable field.

healthcheck package now imports parent pkg/proxy and accepts concrete *proxy.NodeManager, inverting the previous dependency direction and breaking the 'accept interfaces' idiom, forcing tests to build a full NodeManager with a fake clientset.

OnNodeChange godoc says it fires on 'creation or modification' but NewNodeConfig registers only UpdateFunc (no AddFunc); handleChangeNode also carries unreachable tombstone-unwrapping logic; sibling NodeTopologyConfig does register AddFunc, an inconsistent pattern.

NodeManager.OnNodeChange now calls exitFunc(1) unconditionally on any NodeIP change and OnNodeDelete exits on node deletion, broadening prior semantics (previously NodeIP changes never triggered restart, and only PodCIDR change under LocalModeNodeCIDR did) with no feature gate.

The set of 'proxy-relevant' topology labels is hardcoded to v1.LabelTopologyZone in NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent while the actual consumer lives in CategorizeEndpoints (pkg/proxy/topology.go) - a future topology feature must edit two packages in lockstep.
Splitting the fat NodeHandler into a narrow NodeTopologyHandler is strong interface segregation, centralizing the 'changed?' dedup in one place instead of four.
Consolidating getNodeIPs, waitForPodCIDR, NodePodCIDRHandler, and NodeEligibleHandler into one NodeManager removes a second ad-hoc informer factory and duplicated node-watch machinery.
Testability was designed in: newNodeManager/newNodeTopologyConfig inject exitFunc, pollInterval, and pollTimeout, replacing the previous klog.OsExit/panic test hack.
The cross-package invariant between the label filter (NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent) and its consumer (CategorizeEndpoints) is explicitly documented with a linking comment in topology.go.
The OnNodeChange rename is applied consistently across all proxiers (iptables, ipvs, nftables, winkernel, metaproxier, hollow_proxy), and the winkernel no-op + TODO was preserved rather than dropped.

NewNodeConfig's event-handler wiring drops AddFunc entirely while the doc comment for the merged method promises it fires on creation too; old code explicitly had AddFunc: result.handleAddNode which was removed, not folded in.

The informer is now started (inside NodeManager) well before NewNodeConfig/NewNodeTopologyConfig register their handlers in Run(), re-introducing a race the old code explicitly guarded against via a deleted ordering comment.

NodeManager.OnNodeChange overwrites n.node with the new node before validating it has usable host IPs, and NodeIPs() silently discards the lookup error, so n.node isn't rolled back if the new node has no usable addresses.
NodeTopologyConfig.listerSynced is assigned but never read anywhere in the diff, looking like an incomplete port of the NodeConfig sync-wait pattern.

NodeManager.OnNodeChange calls exitFunc(1) for a PodCIDR change without an early return, so if both PodCIDRs and NodeIPs change in the same event, exitFunc can be invoked twice; tests inject a non-terminating mock exitFunc and no test exercises simultaneous changes.
TODO comment references a method name (OnTopologyChanged) that doesn't match the one actually defined (OnTopologyChange).
NodeEligible() takes an unnecessary exclusive lock and unconditionally dereferences hs.nodeManager with no nil check, despite the diff elsewhere acknowledging NodeManager can be nil for hollow-proxy.

Test setup discards the error from proxy.NewNodeManager(...); if construction fails, nodeManager is nil and subsequent calls would panic with a confusing nil-pointer error rather than a clear setup-failure message.
The NodeManager extraction is a clean consolidation of previously-scattered logic, and its own unit tests are thorough, covering existence/IP/PodCIDR wait scenarios and exit-on-change behavior.

Provider interface simplification (config.NodeHandler -> config.NodeTopologyHandler) is applied consistently across all five proxier backends and the kubemark FakeProxier - no implementation was missed.
The new doc comment on CategorizeEndpoints explicitly cross-references NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent's filtering logic, a genuinely useful guardrail against future drift.

Unconditional crash-on-node-mutation is now universal across all DetectLocalMode configurations (not just LocalModeNodeCIDR), and NodeIP-change-triggers-exit is entirely new; before this PR node deletion never terminated the process in any configuration.
The dead cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone-handling branch inside NodeConfig.handleChangeNode is real but harmless defensive code for an unreachable case.
go build, go vet, go test, and go test -race all pass across pkg/proxy/... and cmd/kube-proxy/app with no compile errors, failing/flaky tests, or data races found.

Manually traced that every pre-PR OnNodeUpdate implementation never referenced oldNode and was byte-for-byte identical to OnNodeAdd's body, confirming the OnNodeAdd/OnNodeUpdate merge into OnNodeChange is behavior-preserving.

Traced that the missing AddFunc in NewNodeConfig does not cause a lost initial notification for NodeManager in production, because NodeManager front-loads initial state via a synchronous nodeLister.Get poll before the handler is even registered, making the informer's replayed Add redundant.

Traced topology-label filtering: NodeTopologyConfig does wire AddFunc, so Proxier.OnTopologyChange correctly receives the initial zone label; CategorizeEndpoints callers all consistently switched nodeLabels to topologyLabels.

NodeConfig's informer->handler dispatch has zero test coverage before or after this PR (grep for NewNodeConfig in test files returns nothing repo-wide), and silently diverges from its own doc comment (no AddFunc); if handleChangeNode/handleDeleteNode were no-op'd, no test would catch it.

The tombstone-recovery branch in handleChangeNode and the 'unexpected object type' HandleError paths in both handleChangeNode/handleDeleteNode are also untested; rated low-value since DeletedFinalStateUnknown shouldn't normally reach an UpdateFunc callback.

No negative test exists for watchPodCIDRs=false with a real PodCIDR change (the common-case gating branch); if the watchPodCIDRs guard were dropped/inverted, every non-NodeCIDR-mode kube-proxy would crash-loop on PodCIDR churn undetected.

No test drives OnNodeChange with a node that has an empty/invalid Status.Addresses to confirm no crash and pin down the resulting NodeIPs()/Node() contract, even though n.node is overwritten unconditionally before validation.
TestNewNodeTopologyConfig has no case where a node loses its topology zone label (transition back to {}), which should still fire OnTopologyChange with an empty map per the DeepEqual guard.

None of the NodeManager/NodeTopologyConfig tests rise to brittle/overfit to implementation - they assert on externally observable behavior rather than internal fields and use realistic fake-informer/fake-watch plumbing.
TestNewNodeManager is materially more thorough than the three tests it replaces, including the ordering nuance that a missing NodeIP is reported before a missing PodCIDR even when both are absent.
TestNodeManagerOnNodeChange and TestNodeManagerOnNodeDelete correctly assert on the exitFunc side effect via dependency injection rather than the old klog.OsExit/panic-based customExit hack.

TestNewNodeTopologyConfig correctly distinguishes 'irrelevant label added' (no notification) from 'relevant label added' (notification), and correctly asserts that changing an irrelevant label while the relevant one is unchanged still does not re-notify.

healthcheck_test.go's replacement of hs.SyncNode(...) with nodeManager.OnNodeChange(...) exercises the real NodeManager rather than a bypassed field-setter, more realistic than the old direct-mutation test.

The iptables/ipvs/nftables proxier OnNodeAdd/OnNodeUpdate/OnNodeDelete -> OnTopologyChange migration has no dedicated unit tests either before or after this PR - a pre-existing gap, not something this PR regressed, but worth naming for awareness.

Startup race can permanently disable topology-aware routing on a node: the informer is now started inside NewNodeManager before NodeTopologyConfig registers its handler, causing a data race on eventHandlers and a possible lost initial topology notification, silently disabling PreferSameZone routing.

Topology-label filtering to LabelTopologyZone is safe: routing code (CategorizeEndpoints and callees) reads no other node label, so filtering the node's labels down to just the zone label drops nothing that affects routing decisions.
Node eligibility for health checks (ToBeDeletedTaint / deletion -> 503) is preserved, now computed on-demand from nodeManager.Node(), which returns a deep copy avoiding shared-object mutation.

NewNodeManager guarantees a non-nil node (polls until the node exists with NodeIPs) before returning, so Node().DeepCopy() and the health path cannot nil-deref; hollow-proxy path leaves NodeManager nil but is guarded.

NodeManager.OnNodeChange assigns n.node = node before validating IPs; a transient clearing of status.addresses followed by restoration could produce a spurious exitFunc(1). Requires an abnormal node-status mutation.

Crash-on-change is broadened vs. the old code (exit on any NodeIP change in all modes, and on node delete in all modes, not just PodCIDR change in LocalModeNodeCIDR); appears intentional since a DaemonSet restarts it and kernel rules persist across restart.
handleNodeEvent's doc comment says it handles Add, Update and Delete events, but DeleteFunc is a separate no-op in newNodeTopologyConfig; handleNodeEvent is never called on a Delete event.

Three doc comments (NodeHandler.OnNodeChange interface doc, NodeManager.OnNodeChange doc, NodeManager.OnNodeSynced doc) claim/imply OnNodeChange fires on node creation, but NewNodeConfig registers only UpdateFunc/DeleteFunc - confirmed against client-go source that Add is a no-op when AddFunc is nil.

ProxyHealthServer type doc item 3 still describes the removed SyncNode push model ('sync node status...'), but this PR deletes SyncNode and replaces it with NodeEligible() pulling directly from hs.nodeManager.Node().

TODO(imroc) comment says 'implement OnTopologyChanged' but the actual interface method is named OnTopologyChange (no trailing 'd'); a future maintainer grepping for OnTopologyChanged won't find the method.
The comment '// update the node object' sits above a block that both captures oldNodeIPs/oldPodCIDRs and updates n.node; the comment only describes the last line.
Double space typo in comment: 'If you modify the logic over here to  watch other endpoint'.

NodeTopologyHandler.OnTopologyChange doc comment and its three proxier implementations accurately reflect the AddFunc+UpdateFunc wiring in newNodeTopologyConfig, unlike NodeConfig which doesn't register both.
The comment 'skip calling event handlers when no change in topology labels' accurately describes the reflect.DeepEqual short-circuit immediately below it.

The comment 'hollow-proxy doesn't need node config, and we don't create nodeManager for hollow-proxy' is verified accurate: hollow_proxy.go constructs ProxyServer via a literal, bypassing NewNodeManager entirely.
waitForInvocation doc and the inline callback-explanation comment are accurate and genuinely useful for a reader trying to understand the async test synchronization.

Discarded candidate: the nil-old-node concern from the task hint is actually handled - NodeManager.node is always non-nil after construction, populated synchronously inside newNodeManager before the struct is ever handed to a caller.
Discarded candidate: the double exitFunc(1) call without an early return is provably inert in production because exitFunc is os.Exit, which never returns.

NewNodeConfig's cache.ResourceEventHandlerFuncs literal registers only UpdateFunc and DeleteFunc, omitting AddFunc, so Node Add notifications (including the synthetic catch-up replay for an already-started informer) are silently dropped, contradicting the OnNodeChange doc comment.

NodeManager.OnNodeChange/OnNodeDelete never operate on a nil old node - n.node is populated synchronously inside newNodeManager before the struct is ever handed to a caller or registered as a handler.
handleChangeNode's type assertion correctly falls back to cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone unwrapping, matching the pattern already used for delete handling elsewhere in the file.

The new NodeTopologyConfig correctly wires AddFunc, UpdateFunc, and a no-op DeleteFunc, and safely indexes into a possibly-nil node.Labels map (Go's nil-map read semantics make this safe) - no gaps found there.

metaProxier, iptables/ipvs/nftables/winkernel proxiers, and kubemark.FakeProxier were all migrated consistently to OnTopologyChange; no stray references to the removed OnNodeAdd/OnNodeUpdate/NoopNodeHandler remain anywhere.

Verified no implementation loses diffing information from the OnNodeAdd/OnNodeUpdate merge - the old informer-supplied oldNode was already dead weight in every real caller before this PR; NodeManager.OnNodeChange self-maintains previous state via its own mutex-guarded field instead.

The interface's own doc comment (config.go:263-265) is false for its only production wiring (config.go:288-294, missing AddFunc): NewNodeConfig is the sole outlier among Config types in the file that doesn't set AddFunc, strong evidence of an unintentional regression.

A real gap exists between NewNodeManager (construction, blocking poll) and NodeConfig.RegisterEventHandler (in Run()) during which a NodeIP/PodCIDR change to this node arrives as a synthetic Add (not Update) and is silently dropped, defeating the crash-on-drift invariant NodeManager exists to enforce.

Zero test coverage of the NewNodeConfig/OnNodeChange wiring path: NewNodeConfig is referenced exactly once outside its own definition and never in a _test.go file; existing tests call nodeManager.OnNodeChange directly, bypassing the informer entirely.
Dropping the second parameter and merging Add/Update is evidence-backed simplification, not speculative - the old two-method contract's second value was already dead in every implementer.
Moving the 'is this my node' check from repeated runtime guards into a field-selected informer (fields.OneTermEqualSelector) is a genuine encapsulation improvement.
NodeManager.OnNodeChange correctly self-maintains previous state under its own mutex rather than trusting caller-supplied old state - the right pattern regardless of what the interface provides.

NewNodeConfig omits AddFunc, so OnNodeChange never fires on node creation/replay; NodeManager's crash-and-restart drift-detection safety net silently never fires for changes delivered only via the dropped Add path. 8 of 11 reviewers independently converged on this.

Counter-argument (code-reviewer, verified with build/test/-race, all clean): in the common case the loss is masked because NodeManager front-loads initial state via a synchronous poll before the handler registers, so the redundant replayed Add carries no new information.

Node informer is now started before handlers register (deleted ordering guard), causing a data race on NodeTopologyConfig.eventHandlers/topologyLabels and potentially permanently lost initial topology labels, silently disabling PreferSameZone routing. 3 reviewers independently flagged this.

Counter-argument (code-reviewer): go test -race on pkg/proxy/config was clean, but TestNewNodeTopologyConfig registers the handler before Start() (opposite of production ordering), so the suite never exercises the racy path.

New process-exit behavior is broadened, un-gated, and unobservable: exitFunc(1) is now called on any NodeIP change and on node deletion for every DetectLocalMode, with no feature gate, no metric, and no Kubernetes Event.
healthcheck now imports the parent pkg/proxy and accepts the concrete *proxy.NodeManager, inverting the previous dependency direction and breaking the 'accept interfaces' idiom.

NodeEligible() deep-copies the whole Node under a write lock on every health/liveness probe, though only DeletionTimestamp/Taints are read; wasteful hot-path allocation hit frequently by kubelet liveness and load balancers.

OnNodeChange overwrites n.node before validating IPs, and NodeIPs() discards the GetNodeHostIPs error; if a node update arrives with no usable addresses, n.node is replaced with the broken node and later NodeIPs() calls silently return empty.
The NewNodeConfig informer->OnNodeChange dispatch path - the actual subject of this PR - has no test before or after; a no-op'd handler or the missing AddFunc would go uncaught.

No negative test for the common-case gating branch: watchPodCIDRs=false with a real PodCIDR change; if the guard were dropped/inverted, every non-LocalModeNodeCIDR kube-proxy would crash-loop on PodCIDR churn undetected.
TestNewNodeTopologyConfig registers the handler before Start(), the reverse of production, masking both facets of the informer-ordering race finding.
No test for topology-zone label removal ({zone:X} -> {}), which should still fire OnTopologyChange with an empty map.
Unreachable cache.DeletedFinalStateUnknown tombstone branch in handleChangeNode - only wired to UpdateFunc, which never delivers tombstones. Harmless but misrepresents the handler's event routing.
NodeTopologyConfig.listerSynced is assigned but never read - looks like an incomplete port of the NodeConfig sync-wait pattern.

Missing return after exitFunc(1) in the PodCIDR branch of OnNodeChange; inert in production but fragile given exitFunc is an injectable field, and no test exercises a simultaneous PodCIDR+NodeIP change.
OnNodeChange doc claims 'creation or modification' but wiring omits AddFunc.
handleNodeEvent doc says it handles 'Add, Update and Delete' but DeleteFunc is a no-op.
NodeIPs()/PodCIDRs() docs say values are 'polled in NewNodeManager()', but OnNodeChange overwrites n.node, so they return the current node's values.
proxy_health.go item 3 still describes the removed SyncNode push model (now pull via nodeManager.Node()).
TODO(imroc) says 'implement OnTopologyChanged' - method is OnTopologyChange (no trailing 'd'); a grep for the TODO's name won't find the method.
NodeManager type comment reads like a broken run-on / merge artifact.
Double-space typo in topology.go comment.
healthcheck_test.go discards the error from proxy.NewNodeManager(...); on failure the test panics with a confusing nil-deref instead of a clear setup failure.

The OnNodeAdd/OnNodeUpdate -> OnNodeChange merge is genuinely behavior-preserving: every pre-PR OnNodeUpdate implementation ignored its oldNode parameter and diffed against a self-maintained field, verified across iptables/ipvs/nftables proxiers, NodePodCIDRHandler, and NodeEligibleHandler.

Splitting the fat NodeHandler into a narrow NodeTopologyHandler is strong interface segregation - proxiers now receive only the pre-filtered zone label they consume, and the 'changed?' dedup is centralized instead of duplicated in four backends.
Moving the 'is this event for my node?' check from repeated per-proxier runtime guards into a field-selected informer is a real encapsulation win.
Testability was designed in: newNodeManager/newNodeTopologyConfig inject exitFunc, poll interval/timeout, and completion callbacks, replacing the old klog.OsExit/panic test hack.
The rename is applied consistently across all six proxier backends + kubemark.FakeProxier; no stray OnNodeAdd/OnNodeUpdate/NoopNodeHandler references remain (repo-wide grep = 0).
CategorizeEndpoints gained a forward-reference comment pointing at NodeTopologyConfig.handleNodeEvent's filtering - a good guard against future cross-file drift.
go build, go vet, go test, and go test -race all pass on pkg/proxy/... and cmd/kube-proxy/app (code-reviewer).
